name: Red McKenzie
abstract: William 'Red' McKenzie (October 14, 1899 – February 7, 1948) was an American jazz vocalist and musician who played a comb as an instrument. He played the comb-and-paper by placing paper, sometimes strips from the Evening World, over the tines and blowing on it, producing a sound like a kazoo.

works.t: It’s the Talk of the Town

about_quotes.speaker: Zutty Singleton
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewer mentions Red McKenzie in the context of Dick Slevin, but the interviewee (Zutty Singleton) does not respond or provide any information about a personal relationship with Red McKenzie.

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Carter
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee (Benny Carter) is not mentioned in the transcript segment. The text narrates a story about Red McKenzie arranging a rehearsal for other musicians (Joe Sullivan, Bud Freeman, etc.) who left a previous engagement. about_quotes.speaker: Georg Brunis
about_quotes.evidence: I'll never forget it because Red McKenzie was almost killed with a cab. We were carrying the tables across, you know, in the morning. We were drinking all the beer and helping them move.

about_quotes.speaker: Ram Ramirez
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Oh, I didn't know that. But one thing you said was that Red McKenzie sang with the Spirits of Rhythm at the Onyx. [INTERVIEWEE] That's right.

about_quotes.speaker: Jonah Jones
about_quotes.evidence: He was working up the street from us, in a place called the Swingland, with Red McKenzie

about_quotes.speaker: William Randolph "Cozy" Cole
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Bunny Berigan, Red McKenzie, have you heard of him?
